title: Kundalini and Wisdom: What You Know After You've Been Through It

body:
Kundalini and Wisdom: What You Know After You've Been Through It

You're probably reading this at 3 AM, aren't you? Staring at the ceiling, heart pounding, mind racing. You've been there, haven't you? That feeling like your body's a live wire, your thoughts are a kaleidoscope of terror and revelation, and sleep's a distant memory. I get it. I really do. I've been in that exact spot, more times than I care to count. You feel utterly alone, like you're losing your mind, and no one could possibly understand what's happening to you. But I do. And here's the thing: you're not alone. And you're not losing your mind, not in the way you think.

When you're caught in the grip of something like kundalini awakening, especially when it goes sideways into a crisis, it's the most isolating experience imaginable. You can't explain it to your friends, your family looks at you like you've grown a second head, and doctors often just want to medicate away the symptoms without understanding the root cause. You're left Googling desperate phrases in the dead of night, hoping to find a shred of recognition, a glimmer of hope that you're not completely broken. That's why I'm writing this. Because I've been there, and I want you to know what I know now, after the storm.

Look, what you're experiencing - this intense energy, the strange sensations, the emotional rollercoaster, the sudden shifts in perception - it's real. It's not a hallucination. It's not "just anxiety." It's a deep process of transformation, and sometimes, it's messy. Really messy. It feels like you're being torn apart and put back together again, often without your consent. And that's terrifying. But there's wisdom in that terror, wisdom you wouldn't get any other way. You know?

The Raw Reality of the Experience

You're probably experiencing things that defy explanation. Intense heat, cold, vibrations, electric currents running through your body. Maybe you're seeing lights, hearing sounds, having visions, or experiencing sudden, uncontrollable movements. Your nervous system feels like it's perpetually on high alert. Sleep's a joke, appetite's gone, and every sensation is amplified. You might feel like you're dying, like your body is failing you, or like you're being attacked by invisible forces. Right? I've felt all of it.

I remember one night, I couldn't stop shaking. My whole body was convulsing, not violently, but constantly, like an internal tremor. I was curled up in a ball, convinced my nervous system was just giving up. I thought, "This is it. This is how it ends." But it didn't end. It just kept going, for hours. And then, eventually, it subsided. What I learned later was that my body was releasing stored trauma, energy that had been trapped for years. It wasn't me breaking; it was me The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link). It was a purging.

The Body Remembers Everything

You'll discover that your body holds so much. Not just memories, but emotions, beliefs, traumas. Kundalini, when it moves, stirs all that up. It's like a deep-sea dredge pulling up everything from the bottom of the ocean. You might suddenly feel overwhelming sadness, rage, fear, or grief that seems to come out of nowhere. It's not out of nowhere. It's coming from you, from your past, from unresolved stuff. And it needs to be felt. It needs to be released. You can't bypass it. You can't intellectualize it away. You have to go through it. And that's where the real courage comes in.

I used to try to escape these feelings. I'd distract myself, try to think my way out of them. But it never worked. The energy would just build, and the symptoms would get worse. What I learned, slowly and painfully, was to just let it be. To feel the feeling, no matter how awful, without judgment, without trying to change it. Just observe it. It sounds simple, but when you're in the thick of it, it's the hardest thing in the world. It feels like you're surrendering to annihilation. But you're not. You're surrendering to healing.

The Mind's Betrayal and Insight

Your mind, your trusty companion, can become your worst enemy during this time. It will bombard you with terrifying thoughts: "You're crazy," "You're going to die," "This will never end," "You're a failure." It will create elaborate scenarios of doom. It's trying to make sense of something it can't comprehend, and its default setting is fear. But here's the kicker: your mind isn't you. It's a tool. And you're learning, painfully, to observe it, not to be consumed by it.

I remember having thoughts so dark and bizarre, I was convinced I was developing a serious mental illness. I'd question my sanity constantly. But then, there would be moments of incredible clarity, deep insights into the nature of reality, into my own life, into the universe. It was a dizzying pendulum swing between existential terror and cosmic understanding. You start to see that the mind, while capable of great suffering, is also capable of great wisdom. You're seeing both sides of the coin, raw and unfiltered. It's like reading The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) in real-time, but it's your own damn life.

What You Learn About Reality

You start to question everything you thought you knew. The solidity of your body, the stability of your emotions, the very nature of reality itself. Things you took for granted crumble. The world you lived in before seems thin, almost illusory. You see the energetic currents beneath the surface of everything. You realize there's so much more to existence than what your five senses pick up. It's disorienting, but it's also incredibly liberating.

The Illusion of Control

One of the biggest lessons you get kicked in the teeth with is that you're not in control. Not really. You thought you were running the show, making all the decisions, steering your life. But when kundalini kicks in, you realize you're a passenger on a very fast, very powerful train, and it's going where it needs to go, whether you like it or not. This loss of control is terrifying, but it's also where true surrender begins. You learn to let go, to trust something bigger than yourself, even if you don't know what that "something" is. You learn to ride the waves instead of fighting them. It's not passive; it's a deep, active trust in the process.

I spent so much energy trying to stop what was happening, trying to force myself back to "normal." It was exhausting and fruitless. The more I resisted, the more intense the symptoms became. It was only when I finally collapsed in exhaustion and said, "Okay, do what you're going to do," that things started to shift. It wasn't an instant fix, but it was the beginning of acceptance, and that's a huge step.

The Interconnectedness of All Things

You start to feel it, not just intellectually understand it - the deep, fundamental connection between everything. You're not separate from nature, from other people, from the universe. You're a part of it all. This can manifest as intense empathy, feeling others' pain or joy as your own. It can also be overwhelming, like you're absorbing too much. But it's also a deep truth. You're not an isolated island; you're a wave in a vast ocean. The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) really touches on this, the way your individual experience plugs into something universal.

I remember looking at a tree and feeling like I was the tree, feeling its roots in the earth, its branches reaching for the sky. It wasn't a poetic thought; it was a felt experience. And it changed how I viewed everything. The lines between "me" and "not me" started to blur. It's both beautiful and incredibly disorienting, especially when you're used to seeing yourself as a distinct, separate entity.

The Imperative of Self-Care (The Real Kind)

Forget what you thought self-care was. This isn't about bubble baths and scented candles - though those can be nice. This is about survival. It's about radically listening to your body, your energy, your needs, and honoring them above all else. You learn quickly that pushing yourself, ignoring signals, or trying to perform "normal" will only make things worse. You have to be fiercely protective of your energy, your space, and your peace.

  • Rest is not a luxury; it's a necessity. You'll need more sleep than you ever thought possible. And even then, it might not feel like enough. Don't fight it. Rest when you can, however you can.
  • Food is medicine. What you put into your body matters immensely. You'll likely become incredibly sensitive to certain foods, caffeine, alcohol, sugar. Listen to your body's signals. It's telling you what it needs to support this intense process.
  • Nature is your sanctuary. Being outside, grounding yourself, connecting with the earth - it's not woo-woo; it's essential. It helps regulate your nervous system and provides a sense of peace you can't find indoors.
  • Boundaries become non-negotiable. You'll learn, often the hard way, that you can't tolerate certain people, situations, or environments anymore. Your sensitivity is heightened, and you have to protect yourself. Saying "no" becomes a superpower.
  • Movement, gently. Intense exercise might be too much. But gentle movement - walking, stretching, slow yoga - can help move energy without overwhelming your system.

I had to completely overhaul my life. I couldn't be around certain people, couldn't eat certain foods, couldn't handle loud noises or bright lights. It felt isolating at first, like I was becoming a recluse. But then I realized I was just creating the conditions my system needed to heal and integrate. It was a radical act of self-love, even when it felt like self-deprivation.

The Wisdom of the Aftermath

When the storm finally starts to subside, and it will, you're not the same person. You can't be. You've been through a spiritual crucible. And you emerge with a kind of wisdom that can't be taught in books or gained through intellectual study. It's lived wisdom, etched into your very being.

You'll know, deep in your bones, that life is precious and fleeting. You'll have a deep appreciation for simplicity, for quiet, for genuine connection. You'll see through pretense and superficiality with startling clarity. Your bullshit meter will be off the charts. You won't have the patience for things that don't align with your deepest truth.

You'll also have a deep compassion for others who suffer, because you'll know what true suffering feels like. You'll understand that everyone is fighting a battle you know nothing about. And you'll likely feel a pull to help others, to share your story, to offer comfort to those lost in the dark, just like you were.

But here's the kicker: it's not a destination. It's a continuous process. You'll have periods of peace, and then new waves of energy, new releases, new challenges will arise. But you'll have the tools. You'll have the experience. You'll know how to deal with it because you've done it before. You're stronger than you think. You're more resilient than you ever imagined.

So yeah, it's hard. It's terrifying. It's messy. But you're going to get through this. You're going to learn things about yourself and about reality that most people never even glimpse. And you're going to emerge with a wisdom that will guide you for the rest of your life. Trust the process, even when it feels like it's trying to break you. It's making you. And if you're really struggling to make sense of things, if you think you might be experiencing something called a "spiritual emergency," I'd highly recommend Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link). It was a lifeline for me.

You're not alone in this. I'm telling you this from the other side of countless dark nights, from the quiet calm that eventually follows the storm. Keep going. You've got this.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
